skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, format, and validate Excel workbooks using pandas, openpyxl, and a LibreOffice recalculation helper.
skill.summary: Work with Excel and CSV files, preserve formulas, format workbooks, and verify formula errors.
skill.author: ComposioHQ

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown code fences, Excel formula text, license URLs, and XML namespaces. The confirmed concerns are legitimate but elevated: recalc.py writes a LibreOffice macro into the user profile and runs LibreOffice or timeout commands against a user-provided workbook path.

medium_findings.title: LibreOffice Macro Written to User Profile
medium_findings.description: The recalculation helper creates a LibreOffice macro under the user home configuration directory and writes Module1.xba. This is a persistent application-level change outside the current workbook, so users should understand that running the script modifies local LibreOffice configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly builds a hidden LibreOffice profile path and writes a macro file. The behavior is intentional for formula recalculation, but it is a persistent filesystem modification.

medium_findings.title: External LibreOffice Execution on User Workbooks
medium_findings.description: The helper invokes soffice, and may wrap it with timeout or gtimeout, to recalculate a workbook supplied on the command line. Arguments are passed as a list without shell expansion, which lowers injection risk, but opening untrusted spreadsheets in LibreOffice is still an elevated execution surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are clear and semantically tied to the skill workflow. No shell=True usage was found, so the risk is process execution and document handling rather than direct command injection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation or XML Namespaces
low_findings.description: The URLs in LICENSE.txt point to legal terms, and the URL in recalc.py is an XML namespace inside LibreOffice macro content. No HTTP client, fetch, socket, or upload behavior was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited strings are static documentation and XML metadata. They do not perform network access or transmit data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Code Examples
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md external command detections are caused by markdown inline code and fenced examples for pandas, openpyxl, shell usage, formulas, and JSON output. They are instructional content, not code executed when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown examples and inline literals. The actual executable helper is recalc.py, which was evaluated separately.

low_findings.title: Formula and Spreadsheet Text Misclassified as Malware Indicators
low_findings.description: The C2 and weak cryptography findings map to spreadsheet descriptions, formulas, error names, or ordinary terms. No command-and-control endpoint, cryptographic implementation, obfuscation, or malicious control flow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens occur in benign spreadsheet guidance and examples. I found no supporting semantic evidence of malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Home Directory Configuration Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper writes LibreOffice macro content to a hidden user configuration path. This pattern should be disclosed because it changes application state outside the target spreadsheet.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path and file write are explicit. The risk is environmental persistence rather than hidden malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Execution for Recalculation
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper runs LibreOffice through subprocess.run to recalculate formulas. It avoids shell invocation, but still depends on executing local binaries and processing workbook input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess invocations are directly present and serve the documented workflow. The absence of shell=True reduces command injection concern.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often loses formulas, formatting, or validation when handled by general file tools. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable Excel creation, editing, analysis, and formula checks.
actual_capabilities: Read Excel, CSV, and TSV data with pandas for analysis and summaries.
Create new .xlsx workbooks with formulas, formatting, sheet structure, and column sizing.
Edit existing workbooks while preserving formulas and established formatting conventions.
Use openpyxl for Excel-specific operations such as sheets, cells, styles, and formulas.
Run a LibreOffice-based helper to recalculate formulas and report Excel error locations.
Apply financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and assumptions.
limitations: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice and local command execution.
The recalculation helper modifies the local LibreOffice macro configuration.
The skill provides guidance and helper code, not a full spreadsheet application.
Very large workbooks may require memory-aware pandas or openpyxl modes.

use_cases.title: Financial Model Preparation
use_cases.description: Create forecast workbooks with formulas, assumption cells, color conventions, and error checks before sharing.

use_cases.title: Data Export Cleanup
use_cases.description: Convert CSV or TSV exports into formatted Excel files with readable sheets, summaries, and consistent data types.

use_cases.title: Workbook Maintenance
use_cases.description: Update an existing Excel template while preserving formulas, sheet names, formatting, and workbook conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Workbook
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of an existing spreadsheet.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this workbook and summarize each sheet. Identify important columns, missing values, unusual values, and likely next analysis steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Formatted Workbook
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a new Excel file from structured data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this data. Add clear sheet names, headers, number formats, column widths, and formulas where calculations are needed.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Modify a workbook without breaking its structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this workbook with the new data. Preserve existing formulas, styles, sheet names, and layout conventions unless a change is required.

prompt_templates.title: Build and Validate a Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workbook creation with formula verification.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a multi-sheet financial model with separate assumptions, linked formulas, formatted outputs, and a validation pass that checks for formula errors.

output_examples.input: A sales CSV needs to become an Excel workbook for monthly review.
output_examples.output: A formatted workbook with a clean data sheet, summary sheet, totals, percentage metrics, and readable number formats.

output_examples.input: An existing budget template needs new department data added.
output_examples.output: An updated workbook that keeps the original template structure, preserves formulas, and applies matching formatting to new rows.

output_examples.input: A workbook contains formulas that may have broken references.
output_examples.output: A recalculation report listing whether formula errors were found and where the affected cells are located.

best_practices: Use formulas for calculated workbook values instead of hardcoded Python results.
Preserve existing workbook styles and conventions when editing templates.
Run formula recalculation and inspect reported errors before delivering a workbook.
anti_patterns: Do not save a workbook opened with data-only mode if formulas must be preserved.
Do not replace template formatting with a generic style without user approval.
Do not run the recalculation helper on untrusted workbooks without isolation.

faq.question: What file types does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Excel workbooks and tabular files, including .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, and .tsv files.

faq.question: Can it preserve formulas in existing workbooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends openpyxl workflows that preserve formulas when editing workbook structure and cell values.

faq.question: Can it calculate Excel formulas by itself?
faq.answer: The Python libraries preserve formulas, but LibreOffice is used by recalc.py to calculate formula values.

faq.question: Does the helper change local system files?
faq.answer: Yes. The recalculation helper may create a LibreOffice macro file in the user configuration directory.

faq.question: Is it suitable for financial models?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for assumptions, formula construction, number formats, and finance-oriented color conventions.

faq.question: Should I use it with untrusted spreadsheets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Formula recalculation opens the workbook in LibreOffice, so untrusted files should be handled in an isolated environment.
